display_name: Missing CDP/LLDP neighbor entry does not mean no device is connected — discovery may be disabled, filtered, or using a different protocol
context: Operator checks CDP or LLDP neighbor table and does not see an expected neighbor on a port, or sees no neighbors at all on a connected interface
observation: The interface is up/up and passes traffic, but 'show cdp neighbors' or 'show lldp neighbors' shows no entry for that interface
naive_interpretation: Nothing is connected to that port, or the connected device is powered off or not functioning — investigate physical connectivity
correct_interpretation: CDP and LLDP are optional discovery protocols that must be enabled on both ends to work. A missing neighbor entry has many causes besides an absent device: (1) The remote device does not support or has disabled CDP/LLDP entirely (common on servers, firewalls, load balancers, and non-Cisco devices for CDP). (2) CDP and LLDP are different protocols — Cisco devices default to CDP, most non-Cisco devices use LLDP, and neither understands the other; both must run on at least one side for cross-vendor discovery. (3) The protocol is disabled on the specific interface via configuration ('no cdp enable' or 'no lldp transmit/receive'). (4) A Layer 2 device between them (a non-managed switch, media converter, or patch panel with an active tap) passes data traffic but does not forward or generate CDP/LLDP frames. (5) The neighbor holdtime expired and the entry aged out — this can happen if the remote device's CDP/LLDP transmit interval was increased or if a few packets were lost. Always check BOTH CDP and LLDP tables, and verify the protocol is enabled on both ends before concluding nothing is connected.
why: CDP is Cisco-proprietary — non-Cisco devices (Juniper, Arista, SONiC, Linux servers, firewalls) do not run CDP by default and many cannot run it at all; only LLDP (IEEE 802.1AB) is vendor-neutral
Security best practices often recommend disabling CDP/LLDP on external-facing ports to prevent information leakage — a missing neighbor may be intentional security hardening, not a connectivity issue

Managed switches and some media converters terminate CDP/LLDP — they appear as the neighbor instead of the actual end device behind them, and unmanaged switches/converters neither forward nor generate discovery frames

CDP/LLDP use a holdtime timer (typically 120-180 seconds) — if a few consecutive advertisements are lost due to high CPU, congestion, or brief link issues, the entry ages out and disappears even though the device is still connected and forwarding traffic

confirmation_commands.purpose: Check CDP specifically on the interface in question — absence here means check LLDP next, not that nothing is connected
Check LLDP on the same interface — non-Cisco devices appear here, not in CDP
Verify CDP is enabled on this interface — if disabled, no neighbor will appear regardless of what is connected
Verify LLDP transmit and receive are enabled on the interface
SONiC uses LLDP only (no CDP) — check for neighbors here
Junos uses LLDP — check for discovered neighbors on the specific interface

traps: Checking only CDP on a port connected to a non-Cisco device — non-Cisco devices use LLDP, and the neighbor will never appear in 'show cdp neighbors'
Checking only LLDP on an older Cisco-only network where CDP is the configured protocol — both tables must be checked
Concluding a port is unused because no neighbor appears — the connected device may simply not run discovery protocols; check MAC address table and interface counters instead
Not checking whether CDP/LLDP is disabled per-interface — global enable does not override per-interface disable; 'show cdp interface' / 'show lldp interface' reveals the per-port state

Trusting CDP/LLDP neighbor information as authoritative for physical topology — an unmanaged switch or media converter between two devices is invisible to CDP/LLDP and makes the topology appear directly connected when it is not
